summary: Eclipse MAT identifies a single dominant retention path where the live main thread keeps a local java.util.ArrayList reachable from org.test.HeapDumper.main, retaining almost the entire heap. The retained memory is concentrated in one backing java.lang.Object[], so this looks like a large object graph held by a still-live local variable rather than a dispersed leak across multiple components.

top_retained_objects.agent_note: A single ArrayList local variable in HeapDumper.main retains nearly the whole heap through its backing array.
MAT explicitly flags one Object[] as the main accumulated memory holder under the system class loader.

dominant_allocator_stacks.leak_pattern: A local ArrayList on the live main thread retains a very large backing Object[] and prevents the graph from being garbage collected.

root_cause.description: The org.test.HeapDumper.main method retains a large java.util.ArrayList in a live local variable, keeping its backing java.lang.Object[] and nearly the entire heap reachable.
root_cause.detailed_explanation: MAT explicitly reports that the main thread holds local variables totaling 15,729,608 bytes and points to org.test.HeapDumper.main(HeapDumper.java:23) as the significant frame. The retained set is dominated by one java.util.ArrayList and its backing java.lang.Object[], which together account for 94.84% of the heap. This indicates the memory is being kept alive by method scope on an active thread, so the core issue is an oversized in-memory object graph surviving longer than intended.

remediation: In org.test.HeapDumper.main, inspect the code around HeapDumper.java:23 and stop accumulating all objects into the same ArrayList if they are only needed transiently.
In org.test.HeapDumper.main, clear the ArrayList with clear() and let the reference go out of scope as soon as processing is complete so the backing Object[] becomes unreachable.
Refactor org.test.HeapDumper.main to process items in smaller batches or stream them instead of retaining the full working set in memory at once.
